caveats: a: The occupation mix is for all NAICS 523000 activities and does not isolate exchange operators.
a: Many core exchange functions are already software-delivered, so occupation-based exposure can overstate the remaining automatable share.
rho: Census adoption is not exchange-specific and its highest finance figures refer to very large firms.
rho: FINRA observations concern member firms rather than exchange operators, whose market-infrastructure risk tolerance is lower.
e: The frozen n value is margin-bridged from size classes, not a named list of three verified acquisition candidates.
e: Regulatory venue lists contain legal entities and affiliates that do not map one-for-one to SUSB employer firms or independent control opportunities.
s5: Gallup measures broad owner intentions and not completed regulated-exchange transactions.
s5: Historical large-exchange combinations demonstrate feasibility but may not represent small independent venues or a five-year incidence rate.
q: CME and ICE are diversified global incumbents far larger and more liquid than the LMM lens.
q: Operating margin and revenue mix are evidence about economics, not a direct measurement of retained AI-enabled gross benefit.
d5: The direct metrics come from large diversified exchange groups and may not represent small or emerging venues.
d5: Trading volume and revenue are volatile and do not map one-for-one to constant-quality service demand after fee and product-mix changes.
o: Regulatory status establishes operator accountability but does not quantify migration to substitute market structures.
o: Operator requirement may vary sharply between mature securities and futures markets and newer prediction, digital-asset, or niche contract venues.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include broker-dealers, futures commission merchants, market makers, institutional and retail order-flow providers, issuers, data users, and connectivity customers. Revenue commonly combines transaction and clearing-related fees, access and connectivity charges, market data, memberships, and listings, with demand recurring as customers trade and maintain venue access.
lens.excluded_activities: Dormant or non-operating registrations, proposed venues that have not launched, captive internal matching systems, alternative trading activities classified elsewhere, standalone clearinghouses classified elsewhere, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and exchange operators outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators of recurring physical or electronic external marketplaces that facilitate buying and selling stocks, stock options, bonds, or commodity contracts, including regulated niche, specialist, and emerging exchanges with repeat transaction, access, listing, connectivity, market-data, and member-service revenue.

a.bridge: BLS reports the May 2023 occupation mix for broader NAICS 523000, not six-digit 523210: business and financial operations are 39.70% of employment, office support 19.18%, sales 17.33%, management 15.29%, and computer and mathematical work 6.45%. FINRA identifies financial-industry GenAI use in summarization, information extraction, internal processes, and information retrieval. I map exchange rule research, regulatory filings, surveillance investigation, member support, listings review, market-data operations, documentation, coding assistance, testing, and corporate administration to current AI exposure, while excluding deterministic matching engines, production infrastructure, final regulatory judgments, cybersecurity response, and accountable market operations. Broader 523000 likely overstates brokerage sales and office work but may understate exchange technology and surveillance work; direction is uncertain.
caveats: The occupation mix is for all NAICS 523000 activities and does not isolate exchange operators.
Many core exchange functions are already software-delivered, so occupation-based exposure can overstate the remaining automatable share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupational and payroll table plus role-level time studies from independent exchanges separating production engineering, surveillance, regulatory work, listings, member support, data operations, finance, legal, and administration would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Exchange operators contain many expensive analytical, technical, legal, compliance, and operational workflows suitable for augmentation. The core low-latency matching, control, resilience, and self-regulatory responsibilities are already highly automated or require accountable human and deterministic-system oversight, limiting net new substitution.

d5.bridge: CFTC testimony states that exchange-traded futures and options volume more than doubled over roughly 15 years. In current issuer data, CME's fourth-quarter 2025 average daily volume rose 7% year over year, ICE's full-year exchange net revenue rose 9%, and Cboe's 2025 product results were mixed, including multi-listed options up 24.2%, index options up 20.9%, futures down 4.8%, and on-exchange U.S. equity matched shares up 26.2%. I convert these large-operator and product-specific observations into moderate five-year constant-price demand growth with a downside below one. Large incumbents and volatile one-year comparisons may overstate durable growth for small venues, while emerging products can produce faster growth from low bases.
caveats: The direct metrics come from large diversified exchange groups and may not represent small or emerging venues.
Trading volume and revenue are volatile and do not map one-for-one to constant-quality service demand after fee and product-mix changes.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price revenue, matched volume, open interest, active members, listings, market-data subscriptions, take rates, and liquidity concentration for the named LMM venues, separated between mature and newly launched products, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Electronic participation, risk-management demand, product innovation, global access, data consumption, and new asset classes support growth. Venue failure, weak liquidity, concentration in incumbent networks, fee compression, adverse regulation, product bans, and cyclical trading activity create meaningful downside for an individual LMM exchange.

caveats: The frozen n value is margin-bridged from size classes, not a named list of three verified acquisition candidates.
Regulatory venue lists contain legal entities and affiliates that do not map one-for-one to SUSB employer firms or independent control opportunities.
e.change_evidence: For each of the three frozen LMM-band firms, obtain Form 1 or designation records, audited financials, launch and volume status, ownership and affiliate structure, stand-alone employees, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and control rights.
e.rationale: Operating an external marketplace is inherently a recurring outsourced service, so active independent exchange firms in the frozen EBITDA band generally fit the lens. The SEC and CFTC lists nevertheless show groups of affiliated venue entities, name changes, ownership by large public exchange groups, newly designated venues, and historically dormant or former registrations. Those conditions can leave a firm captive, not independently controllable, pre-revenue, or economically dependent on a larger sponsor even when an establishment is classified in the industry.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 523210 ($1,818,242,554) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 523210 ($16,178,101,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1165 (margins.json[523]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Investments & Asset Management EBITDA/Sales 11.65%); revenue/employee $2,580,239 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 523210); class <5: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $824,550 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $96,060 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,842,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,389,093 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,834,823,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $446,756,909 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3

o.bridge: The Census definition identifies the service as furnishing a marketplace, while SEC and CFTC lists show that securities exchanges and designated contract markets operate through specifically registered or designated entities with ongoing market and self-regulatory responsibilities. I map most surviving exchange quantity to continued need for an accountable venue operator: software can automate user interaction and internal work but does not eliminate the entity responsible for fair access, rule enforcement, market integrity, resilience, and regulated operation. The registration evidence may overstate operator need if activity migrates to OTC internalization, alternative systems, offshore venues, or decentralized protocols, so the downside allows displacement outside the lens.
caveats: Regulatory status establishes operator accountability but does not quantify migration to substitute market structures.
Operator requirement may vary sharply between mature securities and futures markets and newer prediction, digital-asset, or niche contract venues.
o.change_evidence: Market-wide data on exchange versus off-exchange, internalized, OTC, offshore, and decentralized trading shares, plus regulator treatment of autonomous protocols and venue outsourcing, would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: A fully electronic market still needs an operator to maintain rules, access, matching, surveillance, resilience, member relationships, and regulatory accountability. Software can change delivery and reduce labor but is unlikely to replace the accountable operator for most regulated exchange activity within five years.

q.bridge: CME reports $1.3 billion of fourth-quarter 2025 clearing and transaction fees and $208 million of market-data revenue, while ICE reports 2025 exchange revenue split between $3.885 billion transaction revenue and $1.526 billion recurring revenue and a 74% segment operating margin. These large public operators demonstrate fee structures that do not mechanically pass internal productivity gains to customers. I translate the mix of transaction, data, listing, access, and connectivity revenue into relatively strong benefit retention for an LMM exchange, discounting for fee competition, member incentives, regulatory scrutiny of fees, vendor spend, and scale disadvantage. Large incumbents likely overstate margins and pricing power, while niche product differentiation could support retention; direction is uncertain.
caveats: CME and ICE are diversified global incumbents far larger and more liquid than the LMM lens.
Operating margin and revenue mix are evidence about economics, not a direct measurement of retained AI-enabled gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: Target-level fee schedules, liquidity rebates, member and data contracts, regulatory fee filings, customer concentration, renewal history, incremental vendor costs, and contribution-margin results after workflow changes would materially change the retained share.
q.rationale: Most internal efficiency gains can initially accrue through lower operating expense, faster launches, better surveillance capacity, and avoided hiring without changing fee schedules. Over five years, competing venues, liquidity incentives, customer bargaining, regulated fee processes, and reinvestment in reliability and compliance share part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: The Census 2026 AI supplement reports 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, and 50%-60% use among very large firms in knowledge-intensive sectors including Finance. FINRA reports early production use focused on internal efficiency and information extraction while preserving supervision, recordkeeping, communications, accuracy, and fair-dealing obligations. I project those finance-sector signals over five years for exchange support workflows, discounting deployment in market-critical decisions and production systems. Large financial firms likely overstate LMM engineering resources but exchange operators are intrinsically technology-intensive and can use vendor tools; direction is uncertain.
caveats: Census adoption is not exchange-specific and its highest finance figures refer to very large firms.
FINRA observations concern member firms rather than exchange operators, whose market-infrastructure risk tolerance is lower.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of SEC-registered exchanges and CFTC-designated contract markets reporting production AI use by workflow, model-governance approvals, latency and error tolerances, human-review rates, incident results, and net labor hours saved would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Implementation should spread in rule and filing analysis, investigation support, service operations, documentation, software development assistance, testing, incident knowledge retrieval, and corporate functions. Model errors, adversarial manipulation, confidential data, cyber risk, auditability, regulatory approval, resilience requirements, and the need for deterministic behavior limit implementation in real-time matching, market controls, final surveillance decisions, and incident response.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years. CFTC records show actual exchange acquisitions and designation transfers, including NADEX's 2022 acquisition, CME-group and ICE ownership histories, and MGEX designation transfers; the SEC's NYSE-Archipelago record shows that an exchange business combination required approved SRO rule changes and preservation of regulatory structure. I discount the broad intention rate because exchange ownership is strategic, concentrated, and regulator-sensitive, and because intentions are not completed deals. The broad rate likely overstates LMM qualifying transfers, although venue consolidation and sponsor exits make zero inappropriate.
caveats: Gallup measures broad owner intentions and not completed regulated-exchange transactions.
Historical large-exchange combinations demonstrate feasibility but may not represent small independent venues or a five-year incidence rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census for SEC exchanges and CFTC DCMs identifying change-of-control events, designation transfers, sponsor recapitalizations, failed or withdrawn sales, buyer type, approval time, and stand-alone EBITDA would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Control transfers occur and can preserve venue licenses, technology, members, and products, but they require regulator engagement and may involve holding-company or affiliate restructurings rather than a clean acquisition of an independent LMM operator. The tiny frozen target count also makes realized availability lumpy.

principal_driver: The principal driver is the persistence of a regulated accountable marketplace combined with fee models that can retain savings from improved support, surveillance, development, and administration.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is the extremely small, unverified LMM target population in a market dominated by affiliated venue networks and powerful liquidity incumbents.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments furnishing physical or electronic marketplaces for buying and selling stocks, stock options, bonds, or commodity contracts.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 523210 Securities and Commodity Exchanges

sources.supports: Reports 1,064,060 jobs in broader NAICS 523000, including 39.70% business and financial operations, 19.18% office support, 17.33% sales, 15.29% management, and 6.45% computer and mathematical occupations.
sources.title: May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 523000

sources.supports: Reports nationally representative 2026 AI adoption, functional depth, worker-task use, leading text and information tasks, and higher adoption among very large firms in knowledge-intensive sectors including Finance.
sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks

sources.supports: Reports current GenAI implementation for internal efficiency and information retrieval and describes supervision, accuracy, communications, recordkeeping, fair-dealing, and human-oversight considerations.
sources.title: 2026 FINRA Annual Regulatory Oversight Report: GenAI Continuing and Emerging Trends

sources.supports: Lists exchanges registered under Section 6(a), identifies security-futures exchanges using Section 6(g) notices, and notes former or non-operating limited-volume venues.
sources.title: SEC National Securities Exchanges

sources.supports: Lists active designated contract markets and documents designations, ownership changes, acquisitions, affiliate relationships, name changes, and transfers of exchange designations.
sources.title: CFTC Designated Contract Markets

sources.supports: Reports fourth-quarter 2025 average daily volume of 27.4 million contracts, up 7% year over year, $1.3 billion of clearing and transaction fees, and $208 million of market-data revenue.
sources.title: CME Group Reports Full-Year and Fourth-Quarter 2025 Results

sources.supports: Reports full-year exchange net revenue of $5.411 billion, up 9%, including $3.885 billion of transaction revenue and $1.526 billion of recurring revenue, with a 74% operating margin.
sources.title: Intercontinental Exchange Reports Strong Full-Year 2025 Results

sources.supports: Reports mixed 2025 product trends, including multi-listed options average daily volume up 24.2%, index options up 20.9%, futures down 4.8%, and on-exchange U.S. equity matched shares up 26.2%.
sources.title: Cboe Global Markets Reports December and Full-Year 2025 Trading Volume

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and documents the broad survey population.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Documents SEC approval of SRO rule changes needed for an exchange business combination and preservation of exchange and regulatory responsibilities under the new holding-company structure.
sources.title: SEC Approval of Rule Changes for the NYSE-Archipelago Business Combination

sources.supports: States that exchange-traded futures and options volumes had more than doubled over roughly the preceding 15 years amid product, participant, and technological expansion.
sources.title: CFTC Chairman Testimony on the FY 2025 Budget Request

title: Securities and Commodity Exchanges
